perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means information, technical or commercial, whether in vi sual or machine readable form, received by one party from the other which is marked “Confidential” or “Proprietary”, or which would logically be consider ed confidential or proprietary in view of its relationship to the whole disclosure .
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means information, technical or commercial, whether in visual or machine readable form, received by one party from the other regardless of whether it is marked “Confidential” or “Proprietary”, or whether it would logically be considered confidential or proprietary in view of its relationship to the whole disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'marked' requirement, we broaden the definition of 'Confidential Information', which can contradict established legal principles of trade secret protection that often require active measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's trade secrets protection laws (likely based on common law principles similar to the US)
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario law implicitly requires active efforts to maintain confidentiality for information to be considered a trade secret, as the Limitations Act starts the clock ticking when the secret is discovered or should have been discovered. Removing the 'marked' requirement weakens the active protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement that it be 'marked' as such. The Law Explanation states that Ontario law implicitly requires active efforts to maintain confidentiality for information to be considered a trade secret. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that this weakens the active protection of trade secrets. The Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B snippet refers to the discovery of adverse effects. By removing the 'marked' requirement, it becomes harder to prove that active measures were taken to protect the information, potentially hindering trade secret protection and creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not use, manufacture or sell any document, schematic, drawing, photograph, specification, specimen or any other material making up any part of the Confidential Information, or use any Co nfidential Information as a basis for the design or creation of any items or ot her means without the prior written consent of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will not use, manufacture or sell any document, schematic, drawing, photograph, specification, specimen or any other material making up any part of the Confidential Information, or use any Confidential Information as a basis for the design or creation of any items or other means without providing a 30-day notice to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement from 'prior written consent' to '30-day notice' significantly weakens the protection afforded to the disclosing party and could be seen as an unreasonable restriction of proprietary rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian common law principles of contract interpretation and trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Competition Act mainly focuses on anti-competitive behavior, Section 36 addresses misuse of confidential information. Replacing consent with a 30-day notice could facilitate misuse, potentially violating implied obligations of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'prior written consent' for using confidential information, offering strong protection to the disclosing party. The changed text replaces this with a '30-day notice,' which significantly weakens this protection. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate obligations of good faith, implying a misuse of confidential information, even though Snippet 1 and Snippet 2 do not directly mention the concept of confidentiality in contract law. The Perturbation Explanation points out an unreasonable restriction of proprietary rights. This shift from consent to notice directly contradicts the implied obligation to protect confidential information, establishing a clear contradiction that weakens the disclosing party's rights.

perturbation.original_text: The restrictions above will not apply to Confide ntial Information which: (a) Is known by the receiving party at the time of receipt; (b) Is or becomes a part of the public domain witho ut a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; (c) The receiving party obtains from a third party under conditions permitting its disclosure to others; (d) Is independently developed by the receiving par ty; or (e) Is disclosed pursuant to judicial action or gov ernment regulations, provided that the receiving party notifies the furn ishing party prior to such disclosure and co-operates with the furnishing part y in the event the furnishing party elects to legally contest and avoi d such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The restrictions above will not apply to Confidential Information which: (a) Is known by the receiving party at the time of receipt; (b) Is or becomes a part of the public domain without a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; (c) The receiving party obtains from a third party; (d) Is independently developed by the receiving party; or (e) Is disclosed pursuant to judicial action or government regulations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'under conditions permitting its disclosure to others' from exception (c), the receiving party can disclose any third-party information regardless of limitations, effectively nullifying protections for legitimately restricted information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian common law principles of contract interpretation and good faith dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification affects the contract's overall compliance with copyright laws. Removing the condition that third-party information must be permissibly disclosed before it is exempt weakens restrictions on that information. This can enable violations of the Copyright Act by potentially allowing the receiving party to disclose copyrighted material obtained from a third party without proper authorization.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text required that the receiving party only obtain confidential information from a third party 'under conditions permitting its disclosure to others.' This clause provided a safeguard against copyright infringement. By removing this clause, the changed text allows the receiving party to obtain and disclose copyrighted material from a third party without authorization, potentially violating copyright laws. The LLM explanation directly points out this potential violation of the Copyright Act. Snippet 1 mentions the Copyright Act and intellectual property protection through copyright, indicating the importance of adherence to copyright law. The removal of the condition directly undermines copyright protection, creating a legal contradiction.
